coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence enhancement via aggregation effect due to microenvironmental alterations in human hemoglobin protein in presence of carbon quantum dots (CQD): Comparative spectroscopic approach
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chakraborty, Madhurima
Mitra, Ishani
Sarkar, Kuntal
Bardhan, Munmun
Paul, Somnath
Basu, Satakshi
Goswami, Arunava
Saha, Abhijit
Show, Bibhutibhushan
Ganguly, Tapan
coredata.dc:description: CQDs have emerged with outstanding properties as a star member of carbon nanomaterial family and in order to reveal its wide-range of application in biological microenvironment the interactions between human hemoglobin (HHb) and CQD and also with ethylenediamine-functionalized CQD (NCQD) are assessed using several techniques. Firstly, UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb reveal hyperchromic effect in the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues and also hypochromicity of Soret band in presence of CQD and NCQD. Interestingly, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy reveal distinct fluorescence enhancement of HHb with significant red shift thereby indicating exposures of tryptophan and tyrosine residues to a more hydrophilic environment. However synchronous fluorescence spectra reveal that the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues is altered in opposite manner, i.e. exposure of tryptophan residues to a more hydrophilic environment and the tyrosine residues to a more hydrophobic environment. Moreover the fluorescence enhancement is observed to be accompanied by increase in average fluorescence-lifetime and decrease in steady-state anisotropy thus signifying a decrease in restriction of rotational motion. Furthermore tryptophan residues within HHb appear to interact more with CQD compared to NCQD. Thermodynamic parameters as revealed by Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) demonstrate that electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions are the predominant modes of interactions in presence of CQD. Whereas hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions are the major interacting forces in presence of NCQD with five-site sequential binding as best-fit model in both the cases. Such interactions also appear to be associated with an increase in aggregation of HHb as evident from the measurements by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) study. Although FT-IR spectra display alteration of amide I band, but the overall secondary structure of HHb seems to be nearly retained even in presence of CQDs, as evident in the CD spectra. These observations thus highlight the potential biomedical application of CQDs in biological microenvironment of human especially as drug-delivery system. Also bimolecular interaction of HHb as a model protein with other nanoparticles at the nano bio-interface has been outlined.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Human hemoglobin
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence enhancement
Protein micro-environment
Native secondary structure
Biomedical application

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectral investigation
Steady state and time-resolved anisotropy study to reveal freedom in rotational motion
Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy to unravel the alterations in the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues
ITC analysis to evaluate the thermodynamic parameters of the interaction
FT-IR spectroscopy to illustrate the mode of the interaction
CD spectral analysis to elucidate the secondary structural changes
Resonance Light Scattering (RLS) spectroscopy to assess the effect of aggregation
Atomic force microscopy (AFM) to observe the effect of aggregation. Supporting evidences by DLS study
HHb as a model protein for bimolecular interactions with different nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image of CQD (Scale bar - 10 nm).

(a) Representative UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb upon addition of increasing concentration of CQD. The following concentrations of CQD are taken for measurements at ambient temperature - (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. Inset- Absorption spectra of CQD of concentration 3.36 × 10−4 M. (b) Representative UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb upon addition of increasing concentration of NCQD. The following concentrations of NCQD are taken for measurements at ambient temperature - (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. Inset- Absorption spectra of NCQD of concentration 3.36 × 10−4 M.

(a) Representative spectra for steady state fluorescence emission of HHb in presence of CQD. The following concentrations of CQD are taken for measurements at ambient temperature- (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. (b) Representative spectra for steady state fluorescence emission of HHb in presence of NCQD. The following concentrations of NCQD are taken for measurements at ambient temperature - (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M: (λex = 270 nm).

(a) Fluorescence decay curve of HHb (red): (χ2–1.09). (b) Fluorescence curve of HHb-CQD complex (red): (χ2–1.07). Concentration of CQD used here is ~3.36 × 10−4 M. (c) Fluorescence decay curve of HHb-NCQD complex (red) along with the impulse response (faster component shown by the blue line): (χ2–1.07). Concentration of NCQD used here is ~3.36 × 10−4 M. The impulse response (faster component) is presented by the blue line in each decay curve. The residual is also shown at the bottom of each decay curve. (λ ex = 280 nm, λ em = 330 nm).
Time-resolved anisotropy decay of HHb and HHb–CQD complex. Concentration of CQD are (1) 0, (2) ~3.36 × 10−4 M. (λ ex = 280 nm, λ em = 330 nm).

(a) Representative spectra of synchronous fluorescence (∆λ = 15 nm) of HHb in presence of CQD. Following concentrations of CQD are used for measurements (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. (b) Representative spectra of synchronous fluorescence (∆λ = 60 nm) of HHb in presence of CQD. The following concentrations of CQD are taken for measurements (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. (c) Representative spectra of synchronous fluorescence (∆λ = 15 nm) of HHb in presence of NCQD. The following concentrations of NCQD are taken for measurements (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. (d) Representative spectra of synchronous fluorescence (∆λ = 60 nm) of HHb in presence of NCQD. The following concentrations of NCQD are taken for measurements (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M. (e) Changes in synchronous fluorescence intensity ratio of HHb in presence of CQD (∆λ = 15 and 60 nm). (f) Changes in synchronous fluorescence intensity ratio of HHb in presence of NCQD (∆λ = 15 and 60 nm) (g) Changes in synchronous fluorescence intensity ratio of HHb in presence of CQD and NCQD (∆λ = 60 nm).

(a) ITC profiles for the binding of CQD to HHb. The upper panel represents raw results for the sequential titration of CQD (0.33 mM) into HHb (0.005 mM) solution respectively. The lower panel shows the integrated heat results after correction of the heat of dilution against the mole ratio. The data points are fitted to a five site sequential binding site model and the solid lines represent the best-fit result. (b) ITC profiles for the binding of NCQD to HHb. The upper panel represents raw results for the sequential titration of NCQD (0.33 mM) into HHb (0.005 mM) solution respectively. The lower panel shows the integrated heat results after correction of the heat of dilution against the mole ratio. The data points are fitted to a five site sequential binding site model and the solid lines represent the best-fit result.
(a) FT-IR spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of CQD (2). (b) FT-IR spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of NCQD (2).
(a) Representative CD spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of CQD (2). (b) Representative CD spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of NCQD (2).
(a) Representative RLS spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of CQD (2). (b) Representative RLS spectra of HHb in absence (1) and presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of NCQD (2).

(a) AFM image of the HHb. (b) AFM image of the HHb in presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of CQD: Scan area is ~3 μm × 3 μm. (c) DLS profile of HHb. (d) DLS profile of HHb in presence (3.36 × 10−4 M) of CQD, Inset: DLS profile of CQD (3.36 × 10−4 M) (e) DLS profile showing diameter for large moiety of more than one protein molecule.
Fitting parameters of time-resolved fluorescence measurements of HHb in absence and presence of CQD and NCQD (λ ex = 280 nm, λ em = 330 nm).
Parameters of steady-state and time-resolved anisotropy measurements.
Thermodynamic parameters obtained from ITC data of HHb-CQD system at 298 K.
Thermodynamic parameters obtained from ITC data of HHb-NCQD system at 298 K.
Estimation of percentage of α-helix in HHb in presence of CQD and NCQD at 208 nm.
Estimation of percentage of α-helix in HHb in presence of CQD and NCQD at 222 nm.
HHb as model protein for interaction with different nanoparticles.

row.entry: Exposed to a more hydrophilic environment
Exposed to a more hydrophilic environment
Exposed to a more hydrophobic environment
Exposed to a more hydrophobic environment
Exposed to a more hydrophilic environment

head.ce:title: Fluorescence enhancement via aggregation effect due to microenvironmental alterations in human hemoglobin protein in presence of carbon quantum dots (CQD): Comparative spectroscopic approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs have emerged with outstanding properties as a star member of carbon nanomaterial family and in order to reveal its wide-range of application in biological microenvironment the interactions between human hemoglobin (HHb) and CQD and also with ethylenediamine-functionalized CQD (NCQD) are assessed using several techniques. Firstly, UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb reveal hyperchromic effect in the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues and also hypochromicity of Soret band in presence of CQD and NCQD. Interestingly, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy reveal distinct fluorescence enhancement of HHb with significant red shift thereby indicating exposures of tryptophan and tyrosine residues to a more hydrophilic environment. However synchronous fluorescence spectra reveal that the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues is altered in opposite manner, i.e. exposure of tryptophan residues to a more hydrophilic environment and the tyrosine residues to a more hydrophobic environment. Moreover the fluorescence enhancement is observed to be accompanied by increase in average fluorescence-lifetime and decrease in steady-state anisotropy thus signifying a decrease in restriction of rotational motion. Furthermore tryptophan residues within HHb appear to interact more with CQD compared to NCQD. Thermodynamic parameters as revealed by Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) demonstrate that electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions are the predominant modes of interactions in presence of CQD. Whereas hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions are the major interacting forces in presence of NCQD with five-site sequential binding as best-fit model in both the cases. Such interactions also appear to be associated with an increase in aggregation of HHb as evident from the measurements by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) study. Although FT-IR spectra display alteration of amide I band, but the overall secondary structure of HHb seems to be nearly retained even in presence of CQDs, as evident in the CD spectra. These observations thus highlight the potential biomedical application of CQDs in biological microenvironment of human especially as drug-delivery system. Also bimolecular interaction of HHb as a model protein with other nanoparticles at the nano bio-interface has been outlined.

It is apparent that the fluorescence enhancement is accompanied by an increase in average lifetime. Since the lifetime determines the time available for the fluorophore to interact with or diffuse in its environment, thus the tryptophan and tyrosine residues within HHb are probably getting more exposed to the hydrophilic environment in presence of CQD as well as NCQD. Also there are possibilities that the CQD and NCQD can induce motional freedom within HHb as a result of which the tryptophan and tyrosine residues get exposed to the polar environment and such phenomenon in turn may bring about an increase in average lifetime.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Proteins are the most versatile macromolecules in living systems and several key properties enable proteins to serve fundamental functions to a large extent in all biological processes. They are known to bind to several ligands even in nano-dimensions. This may lead to the formation of protein-ligand bi-molecular or more complex interactions where a number of non-covalent interactions such as hydrophobic/hydrophilic interactions, electrostatic interactions, van der Waals interactions produce conformational changes in the protein [1–4]. Thus, the primary understanding of the structural changes of protein in a protein-ligand system is essentially significant as this can precisely assist in the understanding of the role of protein in different realms like biochemistry, medicinal chemistry and nano-biotechnology.

One such important protein which is known to have immense biological significance is Human adult hemoglobin (HHb). HHb is not just a simple oxygen tank rather it is a sophisticated oxygen delivery system. It exists as a tetramer of globin chains, composed of two α and two β subunits, each of which has one redox iron heme as its prosthetic group, and the oxygen binding capacity of HHb is determined by the presence of heme, located in crevices at the exterior of the subunit [1,4–7]. Also Hb has three Trp residues in each αβ dimer, for a total of six in the tetramer: two α14 Trp, two β15Trp, and β37Trp. Among three Trp residues, only β37 Trp is situated at the dimer-dimer interface, which is the region where structural differences between quaternary states are largest [1,4,8,9]. HHb further possesses five Tyr residues in each dimer: αTyr 24, 42, 140 and βTyr 34, 144 [1,4,10]. Moreover the quaternary structure of Hb features strong interactions between unlike subunits [7]. The α1β1 interface (and its α2β2 counterpart) involves >30 residues, and its interaction is sufficiently strong whereas the α1β2 (and α2β1) interface involves 19 residues [7]. Hydrophobic interactions predominate at the interfaces, but there are also many hydrogen bonds and a few ion pairs existing as salt bridges [7]. The binding of O2 causes the heme to assume a more planar conformation, shifting the position of the proximal H is and the associated secondary structure [7,11]. These changes lead to adjustments in the ion pairs at the α1β2 interface [5,6]. Thus it appears that binding of small molecules, including quantum dots, to HHb may produce considerable changes in the overall structure of the HHb [5,6,12–15]. Furthermore it becomes apparent that the physiologically essential protein can also interact with medically important ligands other than oxygen and such interactions may give insight in areas of nano-biotechnology, biomaterials, medicinal chemistry and many more [16–20].

Thus HHb has not confined itself only as a major functional protein for reversible oxygen carrying and storage. It has appeared as a model protein which may undergo significant changes in conformation, function, and dynamic properties after binding of small molecules. Although the relationship between the structure and function of HHb is well studied but the research activity on its interaction with other molecules is limited to a small number of nano-molecules. Based on a systematic understanding of these interactions, one can possibly predict and even regulate the behavior of small molecules of nano-dimension at the nano–bio interface [16,17,19,20]. Vigorous research on these fields may considerably contribute to gain fruitful knowledge regarding their potential in biomedical application especially as drug-delivery system [1–3,21–23].

In this respect the small molecules of nano-dimension that are chosen for investigation are thought to be carbon quantum dots. This may be well imagined since CQDs have gained noble attention as a member of carbon nanomaterial family due to their extensive physical and chemical properties [24–26]. CQDs are nanomaterials with size less than ~10 nm, first obtained in 2004 during purification of single-walled carbon-nanotubes [27]. Since then CQDs have been used in a wide range of applications due to their low cost of preparation and favorable properties such as chemical inertness, biocompatibility, non-toxicity and solubility in aqueous medium [24–26]. These notable properties of carbon-based quantum dots, enable them with potential applications in numerous fields of research [28–40] like bio-imaging [28,29,37,38], biosensor [30,31,34,40], photo-physics [33,35,36] and drug-delivery systems [37–39]. However the application of CQDs in drug-delivery system has not gained much attention. It may happen that the study of protein-quantum dot interactions may open new areas and highlight the understanding of how nanoparticles function in biological environment especially in the fields of drug-delivery systems.

The present work deals with the study of the interactions between HHb (Scheme 1 ) and CQD and also ethylenediamine functionalized CQD (NCQD) by using various spectroscopic techniques. Even though the interaction with CQD and NCQD, alters the microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues in a different manner, but the overall secondary structure of HHb is however nearly retained. Thermodynamic evaluation illustrated that the major interacting forces are electrostatic, hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions but electrostatic interactions do not appear to occur in presence of NCQD. Moreover the hydrogen-bonding interactions as evident from FTIR data and also the effect of aggregation appear to be stronger in presence of CQD system compared to that of NCQD. The above findings thus highlight the biocompatibility of CQD and NCQD in the biological microenvironments. The observations and also a comparative discussion including other nanoparticles are outlined in the following sections.

Human hemoglobin (HHb) (Scheme 1) purchased from Sigma Aldrich, has been used as supplied after preparation of freshly prepared dilute aqueous solution (Millipore Milli-Q system) [1,4]. The final concentration of HHb solution taken for analysis is ~5 × 10−6 M. Citric acid, ethylenediamine and NaOH are purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used for the synthesis without any further purification.

CQDs are synthesized from citric acid by microwave-assisted method described elsewhere [33]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid is dissolved in 10 mL of water. The citric acid solution is then heated in a domestic microwave oven (Whirlpool) at 1200 W power for ~7–8 min and in the course of which the solution color is changed from colorless to pale yellowish brown thereby denoting the formation of CQD. The obtained product after dispersing in Millipore water is centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 1 h. Finally the supernatant collected carefully and purified as mentioned elsewhere and pure CQD obtained are used for experimental studies [33]. The average diameter is observed to be ~2–5 nm (Fig. 1 ). The amine-functionalized CQD referred as NCQD are synthesized by addition of 500 μL of EDA into 10 mL citric acid solution (as mentioned above) [33]. The remaining procedure for preparation of NCQD is similar to the preparation of CQD.

The sizes of CQD [33,40] and NCQD are determined by using High Resolution Transmission electron Microscopy (HRTEM) (Fig. 1), carried out on a FEI, Tecnai G2 F30, S-Twin transmission electron microscope operating at 300 KV.

UV–vis absorption spectra, steady state fluorescence emission spectra, Resonance Light Scattering (RLS) spectra, synchronous fluorescence spectra and FTIR measurements of dilute solutions of the HHb are recorded in absence and presence of CQD and NCQD at the ambient temperature as described elsewhere [1].

Fluorescence lifetimes of HHb and HHb-CQD nanoconjugates are determined by using a time correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) technique with the model FLUOROLOG TCSPC HORIBA JOBIN YVON using 280 nm (NanoLED 280) as impulse for excitation in Trp/Tyr region. The decay kinetics is monitored at an emission wavelength of 330 nm and the quality of fit and tested with a plot of weighted residuals and other statistical parameters (reduced χ 2 and the Durbin–Watson (DW) parameters) by considering the entire decay along with the rising edge [1].

The steady state anisotropy (r) is measured with UV–vis polarizer accessories including UV Linear Dichroic polarizer (Oriel Instruments, USA) and the observed anisotropy values are calculated from the relation given below [4,41].
r =
I VV
−
I HV
I HH
I VH
I VV
+ 2
I HV
I HH
I VH
where I VV denotes the intensities of parallel and I VH denotes perpendicular polarized emission with vertically polarized excitation. I HH gives the intensities of horizontally and I HV vertically polarized emission when excited with horizontally polarized light. I HV/I HH denotes the instrumental correction factor G (polarization characteristic of the photometric system) which is required to monitor any change in the sensitivity of the emission channel for the vertically and horizontally polarized components.

The time-resolved fluorescence decays and polarized emission decays are obtained by the single-photon-timing technique. The time-dependent fluorescence anisotropy function r(t) is given below
r
t
=
I VV
t
−
I VH
t
I VV
t
+ 2
I VH
t

Here I
VV (t) and I
VH (t) respectively signifies the polarized fluorescence decays [4,41].

Titrations are carried out in Isothermal Titration Calorimeter (ITC 200). A typical titration consisted of 20 injections of CQD (0.33 mM) of 2 μl aliquots into a stirred reaction cell (capacity - 200 μL) containing HHb (0.005 mM). The reference power was 10 μcal/s, and the stirring rate was 300 rpm and the temperature is maintained at 298 K. A base-line correction is performed to each experiment by subtraction of data from a series of injections of CQD into water (Millipore Milli-Q system) as blank. The blank data is then subtracted, and using nonlinear least squares method to minimize χ2 values the best fit parameters is assessed. In case of interaction of HHb and CQD/NCQD the best-fit parameters is acquired from the sequential-binding site model. All titrations are performed in triplicate with identical concentrations and injection schedules. The titration curves are analyzed with the sequential-sites by the analysis software (Origin 7.0) installed in the machine.
The Circular dichroism (CD) spectra are monitored using a PC driven JASCO CD spectropolarimeter (Model J-815) in the UV region (250–190 nm) at 25 °C using a 1 mm rectangular quartz cell.

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the samples are acquired by the help of an Atomic Force Microscope XE-70 (Park System, Korea) at ambient temperature. Samples are imaged in Non-Contact mode with a pyramidal shaped long (aspect ratio 4:1) silicon tip. The spring constant of the cantilever is ±40 to 50 N/m and the resonance frequency is ±300 kHz. The collected image data is analyzed by XEI and XEP software (Park System, Korea). Initially 100 μm2 area is taken and decreased gradually to emphasize the average roughness of the particular scan area [3]. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements are conducted by Malvern Zetasizer Nano S (Malvern Instruments Ltd). The backscatter detector of the instrument is 173° and it employs multi-angle (30.1°, 62.6°, 90°) particle size analyzer. The software to acquire and analyze the data is the Dispersion technology software version 7.12.

The results obtained from the above mentioned techniques and presented in the figures are original raw data directly obtained from the instrument and are plotted and analyzed using Microcal Origin (version 8.5) software.

UV–vis absorption spectra of HHb in Millipore water in presence of increasing concentration of CQD has been presented in Fig. 2a. The absorption spectra of HHb without CQD reveal a broad absorption band peaking at ~270 nm. A broad peak around ~270 nm is observed in the absorption spectra of HHb in absence of CQD possibly due to the absorption of aromatic amino acid residues- tryptophan and tyrosine [4,20,43]. In presence of increasing concentration of CQD, hyperchromicity in this region, without any noticeable spectral shift and broadening of the peak is observed.

UV–vis absorption measurements of HHb are then monitored in presence of increasing concentration of NCQD (Fig. 2b). In presence of NCQD also, hyperchromicity at ~270 nm region, without any noticeable spectral shift and broadening of the peak is noticed. Thus the alterations in the spectroscopic properties of HHb (hyperchromicity in presence of CQD and NCQD) are perhaps occurring due to the formation of ground-state complex. While monitoring the UV–vis absorption spectra, the highest concentrations of CQD and NCQD that are used, appear to have negligible absorbance below ~0.02 at around 270 nm (Fig. 2a and b, inset). Since the absorbance of CQD and NCQD at around 270 nm is much low so the corresponding emission spectra are not taken.

The absorption spectra of HHb corresponding to the porphyrin ring also exhibit intense absorbance of Soret band and its occurrence at around at 405 nm usually suggests that the protein is in native form [4,20,42] (Fig. 2a and b). The Soret bands are interpreted as electronic transitions (π-π⁎) within the heme group, which is known to remain embedded in a hydrophobic pocket formed by the proper folding of the protein's backbone [42,43]. A transition from ground state or S0 to the second excited state S2 yields a strong intensity Soret band [44]. Upon addition of CQD there is a decrease in Soret band intensity with gradual broadening of the peak [42,43]. Also concomitant appearance of new absorption band at ~360 nm is noticed (ε band) [42,43]. The decrease of Soret band intensity usually indicates alteration in the chemical environment surrounding HHb perhaps due to perturbation around heme group [42,43]. The development of the 360-nm band upon addition of CQD may be due to perturbation around the central Fe atom [42,43,45]. Also there may be possibilities of variations in the folding pattern of HHb, upon addition of CQD [42,43,45]. Thus the heme environment and consequently the overall integrity of the protein's structure appear to get affected in presence of CQDs. Nearly similar observations are also obtained upon addition of NCQD. Q band of HHb at around 550 nm regime possesses negligible effect.

Thus by far the absorption spectra of HHb showing hyperchromicity in the region of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues possibly suggest the formation of ground state complex. Such interactions may probably affect the overall structure of this multi-subunit heme containing protein and are thus investigated in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectral investigation
ce:section.ce:para: It is already known that a variety of molecular interactions including ground-state complex formation can result in changes in fluorescence properties of the protein (HHb). The fluorescence spectra of the protein (HHb) usually determine the microenvironment of the intrinsic fluorophores (tryptophan and tyrosine residues) and thus appear to be sensitive to the alterations in their surrounding environment within the protein (HHb). Thus fluorescence measurements are performed to check the effect of CQD and NCQD in terms of variations in the neighboring environment of the tryptophan and tyrosine residues at ambient temperature. Fig. 3 displays the steady state fluorescence emission spectra of the HHb that is indicating an emission maximum at ~328 nm upon excitation at ~270 nm. Interestingly the fluorescence maxima of HHb is enhanced regularly at ~328 nm along with an increasing red shift of up to ~10 nm (from 328 to ~338 nm) with increasing concentration of CQD.

Similar observations are obtained also in presence of NCQD (Fig. 3b). Increase in fluorescence emission at 328 nm accompanied with increasing red shift (from 328 to ~338 nm) of up to ~10 nm upon addition of increasing concentration of NCQD are noticed.

It is already known that the emission spectrum of protein is highly sensitive to solvent polarity [1,4,41]. Precisely, an increase in red shift perhaps indicate that the solvent-sensitive fluorescing aromatic residues-tryptophan and tyrosine moiety of HHb, that are perhaps residing in nonpolar hydrophobic cavities are probably getting exposed to a more hydrophilic environment with gradual addition of CQD and NCQD. Notably the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues may get affected in similar or in different manner (discussed in Section 3.4).

To clarify the observation of fluorescence enhancement, time resolved spectroscopic measurements are measured for assessment of the fluorescence lifetimes by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) techniques [4,46] (Fig. 4 ).

The time-resolved fluorescence data for the pristine HHb as presented in Table 1 , exhibit three lifetimes of 0.16 ns, 1.03 ns and 3.4 ns, upon excitation at 280 nm. The fractional contribution of τ3 (0.16 ns) is largest (f for HHb). The lifetime components are 0.27 ns, 1.64 ns and 4.07 ns in presence of CQD and 0.2 ns, 1.46 ns and 3.99 ns in presence of NCQD respectively. There is an increase in τ1, τ2 and τ3 respectively in presence of CQD and NCQD. However there is a decrease in fractional contribution of τ3 (f3 for HHb-CQD and HHb-NCQD) and an increase in fractional contribution of τ1and τ2 (f1 and f2 for HHb-CQD and HHb-NCQD) upon addition of CQD and NCQD.

The following equation is used to measure the average lifetime ⟨τ⟩
(1)
τ
=
∑
a i
τ i
2
∑
a i
τ i

In the above equation a
i denotes the normalized pre-exponential factor and τ
i expresses the corresponding fluorescence lifetime respectively [41]. From the above equation 〈τ〉 for the pristine HHb is found out to be 1.38 ns. From Table 1 it is observed that the average lifetime of HHb increase significantly to 2.37 ns upon addition of CQD and to 2.38 ns in presence of NCQD. Thus, the increase in 〈τ〉 of HHb is comparable in presence of both CQD and NCQD.

Thus it is apparent that the fluorescence enhancement is accompanied by an increase in average lifetime. Since the lifetime determines the time available for the fluorophore to interact with or diffuse in its environment, thus the tryptophan and tyrosine residues within HHb are probably getting more exposed to the hydrophilic environment in presence of CQD as well as NCQD. Also there are possibilities that the CQD and NCQD can induce motional freedom within HHb as a result of which the tryptophan and tyrosine residues get exposed to the polar environment and such phenomenon in turn may bring about an increase in average lifetime [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state and time-resolved anisotropy study to reveal freedom in rotational motion
ce:section.ce:para: As already mentioned that, an increase in average lifetime, may occur due to increase in rotational freedom within the protein molecule (fluorophores getting more exposed due to less imposition of restriction in rotational motion) [46]. Thus the next attempt is to check the extent of restriction inflicted by the CQDs and NCQDs on the dynamic properties of HHb [4]. Anisotropy measurements reveal the average angular displacement of the fluorophore that occurs between absorption and subsequent emission of a photon. This angular displacement is dependent upon the rate and extent of rotational diffusion during the lifetime of the excited state since the time scale of rotational diffusion of biomolecules is comparable to the decay time of many fluorophores [4,41,46]. Since anisotropy measurements appear to measure the dynamics of proteins therefore both steady state and time-resolved anisotropy measurements are carried out.

It is evident from the Table 2 that steady state fluorescence anisotropy (r) decrease upon addition of CQD indicating that the microenvironment of tryptophan and/or tyrosine residues within HHb are probably facing significant freedom in motion upon addition of CQD [4,41]. Thus the observed reduced anisotropy signifies enhancement of the reorientation of the possible sites of interaction (microenvironment of tryptophan and/or tyrosine residues) of HHb in the presence of CQD.

Also additional information, like the values of r(t) can be obtained from measurements of the time-dependent anisotropy and the form of anisotropy decay is usually known to depend on the size and shape and is affected by the segmental flexibility of the macromolecule [4,41]. Thus to check such constraints, time-resolved anisotropy decays are monitored. Fig. 5 shows the time-resolved anisotropy decay of HHb in absence and presence of CQD. From the figure it is observed that the time of anisotropy decay of HHb decreases in presence of CQD. The limiting anisotropy (r0) is also calculated for HHb in absence (r0 = 0.11) and presence of CQD (r0 = 0.12) (Table 2).

Furthermore, correlation times are usually denoted as averages of complex vibrational motions. The measurable distributions of these motions possibly depend on the duration of the lifetimes and also on the structural changes within the surrounding protein [4,41]. The most frequent interpretation of the correlation time is in terms of the overall rotational correlation time (τc) of a protein and this can be obtained by using Perrin's equation,
(2)
τ c
=
τ
r
/
r 0
− r
where r 0, r, and 〈τ〉 are the limiting anisotropy, steady state anisotropy and average fluorescence lifetime respectively [4,41].

The value of rotational correlation time is given in Table 2. It is evident from the table that the rotational correlation time of HHb is reduced significantly in the presence of CQD. The observations possibly demonstrate freedom in conformational constraints that limits the motion of the tryptophan and tyrosine residues thereby generating both rotational diffusion and internal flexibility within HHb upon interaction with CQD [4,41,46]. Nearly similar observations are made in presence of NCQD. These observations are well supported by the other experimental findings [4,41,46].

All the experimental observations, studied in the present investigation, demonstrate increase in rotational freedom around the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues within HHb molecule upon addition of CQD or NCQD. The increase in rotational freedom is observed to be accompanied by steady-state fluorescence enhancement and also an increase in average lifetime in presence of the present carbon dots [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy to unravel the alterations in the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore the steady-state fluorescence enhancement is also observed to be accompanied by a prominent ~10 nm red shift (Fig. 3a and b) which usually indicates an increase in hydrophilicity of the solvent-sensitive fluorescent aromatic residues (tryptophan or tyrosine residues). In order to check whether the alterations of the microenvironment of tryptophan or tyrosine residues are occurring in similar or different manner upon interactions with CQD and NCQD, synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy is performed. The synchronous fluorescence spectra can give insight regarding alterations in the microenvironment of the fluorescing aromatic residues. When a constant wavelength interval (Δλ) between the excitation and emission wavelengths are set at 15 and 60 nm, the synchronous fluorescence provides characteristic information about the changes of polarity around tyrosine and tryptophan residues respectively [1–4,47–50]. Fig. 6a, b, c and d displays the synchronous fluorescence spectra (Δλ = 15 and 60 nm) for HHb upon gradual addition of CQD and NCQD.

Enhancement of synchronous fluorescence intensity when ∆λ is set at 15 nm (microenvironment around tyrosine residues) is noticed in presence of CQD (Fig. 6a). Significant blue shift [51] of up to 7 nm (from 304 to 297 nm) of the tyrosine peak is noticed upon gradual addition of CQD. The blue shift may signify that the polarity around the tyrosine residues is decreased, and thus the hydrophobicity is increased in presence of CQD.

Also increase in synchronous fluorescence intensity is observed when ∆λ = 60 nm (microenvironment around tryptophan residues) in presence of CQD (Fig. 6b). Intriguingly the microenvironment of the tryptophan residues (emission maxima at 337 nm) reproduces a red spectral shift [51] of up to 2 nm (from 337 nm to 339 nm) in presence of CQD. From the red shift it appears perhaps that the polarity around the tryptophan residues is increased, and thus the hydrophobicity is decreased. Thus the microenvironment of both the tyrosine and tryptophan residues is significantly as well as differently or oppositely altered in presence of CQD.

The observations in presence of NCQD (Fig. 6c and d) also appear to be nearly comparable to that in presence of CQD. Here also the blue shift that is observed in Fig. 6c may denote that the polarity around the tyrosine residues is decreased, and thus the hydrophobicity is increased in presence of NCQD. The red shift that is observed in Fig. 6d probably indicates that the polarity around the tryptophan residues is increased, and thus the hydrophobicity is decreased. Thus the microenvironment of both the tyrosine and tryptophan residues is significantly and to some extent oppositely altered in presence of NCQD also like the situations in presence of CQD.

The fluorescence intensity ratio is also assessed by setting constant wavelength at both 15 and 60 nm (Fig. 6e and f). The ratios appear to be more when ∆λ is set at 60 nm intervals in presence of both CQD (Fig. 6e) and NCQD (Fig. 6f). It may happen that CQD and NCQD appear to make closer interaction to tryptophan residues compared to tyrosine residues. Importantly, this observation is going in favor with the previous observation that the microenvironment of tryptophan residues is exhibiting a red shift or getting exposed to a polar environment (Fig. 6b and d). Also the interaction of tryptophan residues with CQD is more prominent in nature compared to that with NCQD (Fig. 6g).

Moreover it becomes apparent that the involvement of the indole N atom of tryptophan residue and the hydroxyl part of the tyrosine residue possibly facilitates the microenvironmental changes within the structure of the HHb due to interaction with CQD and NCQD. Here the red shift of the tryptophan peak signifies that the tryptophan residues embedded in the nonpolar hydrophobic environment are exposed to a comparatively hydrophilic environment. The microenvironment of the tyrosine residues is altered perhaps in an opposite manner. Significant blue shift that is observed in case of tyrosine peak indicates that the tyrosine residues present in the hydrophilic environment is perhaps buried into a hydrophobic environment. Thus conformational changes of HHb are possibly occurring in terms of alteration at the surrounding microenvironment of fluorescing aromatic residues due to the binding of both CQD and NCQD.

In particular, the steady-state fluorescence showing significant red shift may indicate increase in hydrophilicity of aromatic residues. Whereas synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy reveals that the predominant solvent-sensitive fluorescing aromatic residues- tryptophan and tyrosine are getting affected in opposite manner at least to some extent. Specifically tryptophan residues are getting exposed to a hydrophilic environment (red shift) and tyrosine residues are getting exposed to a more hydrophobic environment (blue shift). Thus alterations in protein microenvironment are noticed and the interacting forces leading to such changes needs to be evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ITC analysis to evaluate the thermodynamic parameters of the interaction
ce:section.ce:para: The nature of interaction of HHb with CQD and NCQD is then explored by performing ITC [51]. The purpose to perform this calorimetric study is to determine the thermodynamics of interactions of HHb with CQD and NCQD, there by finding the overall nature of the forces involved in the interaction. Fig. 7 shows the representative calorimetric profiles of the titration of CQD and NCQD with HHb. Each heat burst curve is corrected by subtracting the corresponding heat of dilution obtained from control experiments by injecting CQD or NCQD into the water (Millipore Milli-Q system) alone. The resulting corrected values of the binding are plotted against the molar ratio and the continuous lines in the lower panels are the heat changes that are best fitted by a five-site sequential binding model (Fig. 7a and b).

As observed in Fig. 7a, in presence of CQD, the integrated heats of injection show a tendency towards initial increasingly negative enthalpy (exothermic heats), then towards increasingly positive enthalpy (endothermic heats). Furthermore a decrease in enthalpy and finally increasingly negative enthalpy (exothermic heats) is observed until saturation is reached. The thermodynamic parameters obtained from ITC experiments are displayed in the Table 3a . It appears that the binding constant also almost gradually decreases for five-site sequential binding. Moreover the binding is driven by higher negative enthalpic value for third and fifth binding sites. However the binding is predominantly driven by high positive enthalpic value for fourth binding sites. The enthalpy values are comparatively lesser for the first and second sequential binding. However the free energy change is negative (∆G < 0), in all cases thereby indicating spontaneous interaction for all five-site sequential binding. The values of ∆G are determined from Gibbs–Helmholtz equation as follows,
(3)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S

The entropic contribution also appears to be comparatively higher with positive value for fourth and higher negative value for the fifth binding site, implying that the interactions are spontaneous and both entropy and enthalpy driven, at the fourth and fifth binding site. The comparatively larger entropic contribution further suggests conformational changes and alteration in the microenvironment of multi-subunit HHb upon interaction with CQD.

The above observations also indicate that the interacting forces are likely to be electrostatic (highest binding constant, K1 - 3.14 × 106 M−1), hydrophobic interactions (binding constant, K2 - 7.14 × 105 M−1 and K4 4.86 × 104 M−1) as well as hydrogen bonding (binding constant, K3 - 2.4 × 105 M−1 and K5 - 1.95 × 104 M−1) (Table 3a). From Table 3a it appears that initially there is electrostatic interaction (∆H < 0 and ∆S > 0) probably involving the positively charged residues of HHb in presence of CQD for the first binding site. Such interactions are probably causing certain conformational changes in HHb thereby facilitating hydrophobic interactions (∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0) for HHb-CQD system for the second and fourth binding site. These alterations within the multi-subunit HHb are furthermore followed by hydrogen bond and van der Waals interaction(∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0), for the third and fifth binding site, suggesting some changes in protein microenvironment in presence of CQD. This can be well imagined since it will be observed in the next section that changes in IR absorption intensity of the CO stretching vibration is occurring due to the participation of the CO group of the polypeptide chain in hydrogen bond formation (Fig. 8a). During all such interactions the microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues are also perhaps getting affected which has already been reflected in steady state (Fig. 3a) and synchronous fluorescence spectroscopic observations (Fig. 6a and b).

In case of NCQD, the isotherm profile of heat changes versus molar ratio of NCQD for HHb is also best fitted (least χ2 values) to a five-site sequential binding model (Fig. 7b). Here the binding is observed to be an exothermic process. The integrated heats of injection show a tendency towards initial increasingly negative enthalpy, then towards decreasing negative enthalpy until saturation is reached. The thermodynamic parameters obtained from ITC experiments are shown in the Table 3b . The binding constant initially decreased (K′1 - 3.67 × 105 M−1 and K′2 - 2.02 × 104 M−1) for the second binding site, then gradually increased for the third and fourth binding site (K′3 - 1.02 × 105 M−1 and K′4 - 2.79 × 105 M−1) and finally again decreased at the fifth binding site (K′5 - 1.2 × 105 M−1) considering the five-site sequential binding. Also the binding is driven by negative enthalpy value for second, fourth and fifth binding sites and by positive enthalpic value for first and third binding sites. However the free energy change remains negative (∆G < 0) in all cases, thereby indicating, spontaneity of interactions for all five-site sequential binding, in presence of NCQD. Although the binding appeared to be enthalpy driven for second, third, fourth and fifth binding site, but the comparatively higher positive entropy value for third binding site and higher negative entropy value for fifth binding site and closer enthalpy and entropy value for first binding site suggests that these bindings are both enthalpy and entropy driven. The large entropy contribution also suggests conformational changes and alteration in the protein microenvironment upon interaction with NCQD.

Thus the above observations indicate that the interacting forces are likely to be hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions. From Table 3b it appears that initially the interaction between HHb and NCQD is hydrophobic (∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0) in nature (highest binding constant K′1). These hydrophobic interactions are facilitating weak hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interaction (∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0) with decreasing binding constant (K′2). The third sequential binding in presence of NCQD, may promote alterations in protein microenvironment due to hydrophobic interactions (∆H > 0 and ∆S > 0) with stronger binding constant (K′3). The fourth and fifth sequential binding (K′4 and K′5) are occurring possibly through further hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interaction (∆H < 0 and ∆S < 0) suggesting some conformational changes in the protein microenvironment. This is going in well agreement with our earlier observations where alterations in the microenvironment around tryptophan and tyrosine residues have been observed in steady state (Fig. 3b) and synchronous fluorescence spectroscopic data (Fig. 6c and d). The involvement of the CO group of the polypeptide chain in hydrogen bond formation in presence of NCQD will also be observed in the FTIR spectroscopy data discussed in the following section (Fig. 8b).

Moreover the highest value of binding constant appears to be more for CQD (K1 - 3.14 × 106 M−1) compared to that of NCQD (K′1 - 3.67 × 105 M−1). This observation can be well realized further from Fig. 6g which also denotes that the interaction of HHb and its tryptophan residues with CQD is more prominent in nature compared to that with NCQD. However there is a gradual decrease in binding constant for five-site sequential binding upon interaction with CQD but there is no such tendency in presence of NCQD. Rather a nearly oscillating decrease and increase in binding constant are observed upon interaction with NCQD. Also HHb shows strong electrostatic interactions in presence of CQDs probably involving positively charged residues of HHb and this type of interaction is not observed after doping of CQD (NCQD).

Therefore the systematic efforts reveal a five-site sequential binding model to be the best-fit model for the interaction of multi-subunit HHb with CQD and NCQD. Also, the five-site sequential binding can be expected for the multi-subunit protein HHb containing two α and two β subunits and such binding may lead to alteration in dimer-dimer interface or in the individual subunits. Furthermore evaluation thermodynamic parameters also suggest the nature of interaction between HHb and CQDs. From the observations it appears that electrostatic, hydrogen bond and hydrophobic interactions are the major interacting forces in presence of CQD with electrostatic interactions at the first sequential binding site having the strongest binding constant (K1). Whereas in presence of NCQD the hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions are the major driving forces with hydrophobic interactions at the first sequential binding site having the strongest binding constant (K′1). Also the highest binding constant (K1) for CQD is observed to be higher compared to the highest binding constant for NCQD(K′1) thereby indicating stronger interaction of HHb with CQD than with NCQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectroscopy to illustrate the mode of the interaction
ce:section.ce:para: Thus it becomes clear now that electrostatic, hydrophobic as well as hydrogen bonding are the major modes of interacting forces between HHb and CQDs. Such interactions may occur due to conformational changes in the structure of the protein which may lead to alterations in the pairing pattern of hydrogen bonds within the protein backbone as well as in its polar side-chains. Therefore in order to check whether such non-covalent interactions are interacting with the polypeptide backbone especially through hydrogen bonding interactions FTIR spectroscopy has been performed. In this case, conformational changes due to interactions with CQDs can be sensed by spectral properties of the infrared region keeping in mind the carbonyl stretching vibration within HHb (Fig. 8).

The CO and CN stretching vibrations of peptide linkages can be monitored by amide I band of the infrared absorption region at around 1700–1600 cm−1 [1,4,42,43,52-54]. As depicted in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 8a), CO stretching vibration of HHb is noted at 1643 cm−1. Upon interaction with CQD, the IR absorption intensity is decreased but with no spectral shift. Thus the changes in the amide I band in the infrared absorption region possibly demonstrate the involvement of the CO group of the polypeptide backbone in hydrogen bond formation. Here the indole N atom of tryptophan moiety may play active role in such hydrogen bonding interaction. This can be well imagined since synchronous fluorescence measurements are also indicating exposure of tryptophan residues to more hydrophilic environment and thus probably facilitating more of such interactions with it.

Again Fig. 8b displays the FT-IR spectra of HHb upon interaction with NCQD. Here also IR absorption intensity for CO stretching vibration is observed to decrease. However the decrease in IR intensity of HHb is much less in presence of NCQD compared to that of CQD. These changes in the IR absorption intensity of the CO stretching vibration again suggest the participation of the CO group of the polypeptide chain in hydrogen bond formation probably with the N-atom of the indole group of tryptophan moiety. However it may happen that the involvement of the CO group of the polypeptide chain in hydrogen bond formation with the N-atom of the indole group of tryptophan moiety is more in presence of CQD compared to NCQD.

Whether the tyrosine residues which is probably getting exposed to a more hydrophobic environment is having any specific involvement in the H− bonding interactions is beyond the scope of this study. Also the larger infrared intensity of amide I band must have masked the amide II band [1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD spectral analysis to elucidate the secondary structural changes
ce:section.ce:para:
From the above observations it is realized that CO group of the polypeptide chain is interacting probably with the N-atom of the indole group of tryptophan moiety through hydrogen bond formation. Such interactions will probably also affect the overall secondary structure corresponding to the backbone of HHb. To check the conformational change of protein at the secondary structural level CD spectroscopy is performed [1,4,55–57]. Fig. 9 shows a positive peak at ~195 nm and negative peaks at about 208 and 222 nm. These peaks in the CD spectra of proteins correspond to α-helical secondary structure [1,4,20,42,43]. As evident in the figure, there is a slight increase in the negative bands at 208 and 222 nm and a slight decrease in the positive peak at 196 nm in presence of CQD and NCQD (Fig. 9a and b). The following equation is used to express the CD spectral observations in terms of mean residue ellipticity (MRE) (degree cm2 mol−1),
(4)
θ λ
=
θ
10 nlc

Here c indicates the concentration of protein (g/mL), θ indicates observed rotation (degree), l indicates the path length (cm) and n indicates the number of amino acid residues of HHb (574 amino acid residues).

The following equation is utilized to evaluate the percentage of α-helicity in HHb upon interaction with CQD and NCQD,
(5)
% of
α − helix =
−
θ
208
− 4000
33000 − 4000
(6)
% of
α − helix =
θ
222
+ 2340
− 30300

Table 4a presents percentage of α-helical content of HHb is ~24.5% as calculated from Eq. (5). The percentage of α–helicity slightly increases to ~26.5% in presence of CQD and slightly decreases to ~24.1% in presence of NCQD. Thus the CD spectra of HHb at 208 nm nearly retain its α -helical secondary structure even in the presence of CQD and NCQD.

Again using Eq. (6) the percentage of α-helical secondary structure of HHb is assessed and summarized in Table 4b . The negative band at 222 nm is a more selective probe for the α–helicity and it originates from the n-π* amide transition of peptide chain and interference caused by other secondary structures are relatively weak at this wavelength. Table 4b shows that HHb had ~36.5% α-helicity in the absence of CQD and NCQD at 222 nm. The percentage of α–helicity decreases from ~36.5% to ~31.3% in presence of CQD. Likewise the α–helicity decreases from ~36.5% to 30.4% in presence of NCQD at 222 nm.

Since minute changes in the values of mean residue ellipticity (MRE), θ upon addition of CQD and NCQD is observed, thus indication of retention of overall secondary structure of HHb can be realized. On the whole, CQD and NCQD may emerge as a biocompatible star member of carbon nanomaterial family as they can be utilized, as biosensors, in bio-imaging and especially as drug-delivery system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resonance Light Scattering (RLS) spectroscopy to assess the effect of aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: The next attempt is then to assess whether all the above mentioned changes are promoting any consequence of aggregation of HHb upon interaction with CQD and NCQD. To check this RLS [1,3,58,59], AFM [1,3] and DLS [16] studies are performed.

Fig. 10 shows the RLS spectra of HHb. In presence of CQD, increase in RLS intensity is noticed (Fig. 10a). Since an increase in RLS intensity corresponds to increase in particle dimension of the formed aggregate in solution thus it appears that interaction with CQD may promote aggregation of HHb and such interpretations needed further investigation.

RLS spectra of HHb are also monitored in presence of NCQD and it remains nearly unchanged (Fig. 10b). Thus interaction with NCQD is not facilitating any noticeable effect of aggregation and thus there is no visible increase in light scattering signal.
The above observations thus indicate that the effect of aggregation appears to be more upon interaction with CQD compared to that for NCQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy (AFM) to observe the effect of aggregation. Supporting evidences by DLS study
ce:section.ce:para: AFM experiments are then performed to monitor the properties of aggregation upon interaction of HHb and CQD by using the tapping-mode in the air. Since tapping mode is used for imaging, some distortions may occur in the form of exaggerated size features in the sample. Fig. 11a shows the image of HHb and Fig.11b exhibits the image of HHb in presence of CQD. From the images it is clearly observed that a number of CQDs are involved to form stable interactions. The images also highlight about the aggregation of more than one protein molecule adjacently staying together and such interactions are stabilized by the CQD (Fig. 11b). Some aggregations also form complexes of size as big as 600 nm which are also supported by the DLS study, discussed in the next section. Smaller structures of below ~120 nm are also observed which indicates the bare HHb protein molecules and the molecules with height of <40 nm are probably aggregated CQD molecules which do not interact with the protein. Thus, the morphological characteristics obtained by AFM topographical study illustrates that specific interaction occurs upon complexation of HHb with CQD.

DLS studies are then performed and Fig. 11c reveals an approximate size of 115 ± 3 nm which may correspond to diameter size of the single HHb molecule in normal Milli-Q water (pH – 7) at room temperature. The HHb-CQD complex shows a diameter of 121.5 nm ± 2.3 indicating probably the interaction of single CQD and HHb (Fig. 11d). The DLS profile of CQD has been presented in the inset of Fig. 11d which displays the diameter of CQDs to be ~7.4 nm. Some complexes represent a diameter of above ~600 nm which is seen as well in the AFM image and probably giving an insight that more than one protein molecule (Fig. 11e) aggregate together and are stabilized by CQD molecules.

Thus the above observations suggest that CQD (and functionally doped NCQD) that are emerging as a crucial member of carbon nanomaterial family, by virtue of their distinctive physicochemical, optical and electronic properties, can have potential applications in numerous fields such as drug-delivery system, bio-sensing and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HHb as a model protein for bimolecular interactions with different nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous work we also observed the nature of interactions of HHb with gold nanoparticles of two different morphologies (spherical and star-shaped) [1]. With respect to this, a brief discussion regarding the interaction of HHb with different nanoparticles examined has been outlined in the last section of this article and summarized in Table 5 . Since we have discussed the interaction of HHb as a model protein with four different nanoparticles of two different families gold and carbon nano family, so a summarization in a tabular form, will give distinct idea regarding their corresponding interactions. Also a detailed insight regarding, how the same model protein interact with different nanomaterials can be obtained. This is because different nanomaterials have different physicochemical and electronic properties based on their structure, as such the nanomaterials are known to have varied unique properties. Thus if proper knowledge is gathered, the nanoparticles can be utilized in wide-range of applications. The potential of such nanoparticles in the biological realm however cannot be fundamentally realized unless their interaction whether stable or unstable is assessed with biological macromolecules of immense physiological significance like HHb. Thus it appears that the interaction of HHb with different nanomaterials if understood properly at the nano-biointerface, it will definitely give some hints regarding the particular field of application of nanoparticles. Keeping all these in mind the following analysis has been performed regarding interaction of HHb with members of gold nanofamily (gold nanoparticle - GNP, gold nanostar - GNS) and carbon nanofamily (CQD and NCQD). The comparative approach can be distinctly helpful if represented in a tabular form that has been given below (Table 5).

UV–vis electronic absorption spectra of HHb reveal a prominent hyperchromic effect for GNP, CQD and NCQD but a weak yet reproducible hypochromic effect in presence of GNS. Although the observations suggest the formation of ground-state complex upon interaction with all four nanoparticles but the change in the regions of absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine residues is different.

However more interesting observations are noticed for the steady state fluorescence emission spectra of HHb. In case of noble nanometal gold (GNP and GNS) fluorescence quenching of static type without any spectral shift is observed. Intriguingly fluorescence enhancement with significant red shift of ~10 nm of HHb in presence of quantum dots is noticed. This red shift indicates exposure of tryptophan and tyrosine residues to a more hydrophilic environment. Also another stimulating observation is that the magnitudes of the τ of HHb remain nearly similar in presence of gold nanoparticles of two different shapes (GNP and GNS). However the magnitude of τ of HHb, increases significantly (~1.7 times) upon addition of carbon quantum dots (CQD and NCQD). Thus GNP and GNS upon interaction with HHb are altering the microenvironment of the fluorescing aromatic residues tryptophan and tyrosine (static quenching with no spectral shift) in a different manner compared to that of CQD and NCQD (fluorescence enhancement with red shift of ~10 nm).

Moreover synchronous fluorescence intensity of HHb shows a decrease for the tyrosine (∆λ = 15 nm) peaks with weak red shift (from 304 to 305 nm) in presence of GNP and nearly remaining unchanged in presence of GNS. The red shift is perhaps indicating exposure of tyrosine residues to a more hydrophilic environment in presence of GNP. However the synchronous fluorescence intensity of HHb gradually decreases without any spectral shifts for the tryptophan residues (∆λ = 60 nm) upon addition of both GNP and GNS.

As expected there is an increase in synchronous fluorescence intensity in presence of both CQD and NCQD. Surprisingly the tyrosine peak shows an intense blue shift of ~7 nm while the tryptophan peak displays red shift of ~2 nm. Thus the possible interaction of HHb with CQD and NCQD probably exposing tryptophan residues to a more hydrophilic environment and unlike noble nanometal gold the tyrosine residues are getting exposed to a more hydrophobic environment. Different nanomolecules (GNP, GNS and CQD, NCQD) after interacting with the same model protein (HHb) are altering the microenvironment of HHb differently such that the tryptophan and tyrosine residues are getting exposed to either a hydrophilic or hydrophobic environment. These observations may be expected since the nanoparticles vary in size, shape, physicochemical and photophysical properties from each other.

Since the microenvironment of tyrosine residues are getting exposed to hydrophilic environment in presence of GNP, therefore it may happen that the gold nanoparticles are facilitating hydrogen bonding interactions by involving tyrosine hydroxyl group and CO group of HHb. Whereas the CQDs are involving indole N atom of tryptophan residues and CO group of HHb as evident in the synchronous and FT-IR spectra perhaps due to exposure of tryptophan residues to a more hydrophilic environment.
Such interactions of GNP, GNS and CQD, NCQD are also facilitating stable interaction of individual HHb to enhance protein aggregation.

Strikingly in spite of the alterations observed in the microenvironment of HHb, upon its interaction with GNP, GNS and CQD, NCQD, the overall secondary structure of HHb as revealed by CD spectroscopy, is however more or less retained. Thus interactions of these biocompatible nanoparticles at the nano bio-interface can be widely utilized in wide-range of fields. Whether the nanoparticles will be used as an efficient drug-delivery system, biosensor or bioimaging and other biological fields will then depend on the properties of the nanoparticles and their nature of interactions. Thus it becomes apparent that different nanomaterials can affect the spectroscopic properties of the same biomolecule HHb in their own way. All in all the systematic efforts have revealed the role of HHb as a model protein in bimolecular interaction (Table 5). Knowledge regarding the potential applications of different nanomaterials can be obtained by monitoring the nature of interactions between them.

The present spectroscopic investigations reveal the nature of interactions of biologically significant protein HHb with the emerging member of carbon nanomaterial family-CQDs. UV–vis absorption data reveal hyperchromic effect at 270 nm due to absorbance by tryptophan and tyrosine indicating the possibility of formation of ground state complex. Steady-state fluorescence enhancement with prominent spectral red shift although indicate exposure of tryptophan and tyrosine residues to a more hydrophilic environment in presence of CQD and NCQD but the synchronous fluorescence spectra however reveal that the microenvironment of tryptophan and tyrosine appear to be altered in different manner such that the tyrosine residues are getting exposed to a more hydrophobic environment and the tryptophan residues to a more hydrophilic environment. Moreover the fluorescence enhancement seems to be associated with increase in average lifetime perhaps due to increase in rotational freedom. Evaluation of thermodynamic parameters from ITC study illustrates that electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions are the predominant modes of interaction in presence of CQD whereas hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions are the major interacting forces in presence of NCQD. Moreover considering all the sequential binding sites, the binding constant is highest for CQD. Also the CQDs appear to interact more with the tryptophan residues of HHb and accordingly are observed to promote an increased aggregation of HHb. Here the tryptophan residues in the hydrophilic environment may involve the N-atom of its indole ring to form hydrogen bonding interactions with the CO group of the polypeptide chain. Whether such effect of enhanced aggregation of HHb is having any significance in clinical biochemistry, biomaterials and nano-biotechnology needs to be explored. However in spite of such alterations in microenvironment of HHb in presence of CQDs, the overall secondary structure of the HHb is retained as revealed in CD spectral analysis. The present investigation hints that though there are several type of interactions (electrostatic, H-bonding, hydrophobic) between HHb and CQDs, but the safety of the protein environment retains.

Largely the above observations determine the interaction of HHb with CQD and NCQD and suggest the potential applications of CQDs as drug delivery systems, biosensors and in other areas in human as well. Also the underlying role of HHb as a model protein in bimolecular interaction has been highlighted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: TG gratefully acknowledges the University grant commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for awarding Emeritus Fellowship and providing contingency grant (F.6-6/2014-15/EMERITUS-2014-15-GEN-3976) for research purpose. MB expresses her sincere gratitude to the DST-SERB project YSS/2015/000589, DST, New Delhi, Govt of India. We deeply acknowledge Prof. Maitrayee DasGupta and Dr. Sanghamitra Sengupta of Department of Biochemistry, University of Calcutta, for helping to perform ITC experiments. Special thanks to Ms. Sharmistha Ghosal, Mr. Souvik Roy and Mr. Arijit Pal (DBT-IPLS, University of Calcutta) for their help in ITC analysis. We are grateful to the DBT-IPLS facility, University of Calcutta for supporting to perform ITC experiments.
